coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric sensing of polar E120 in juice and environmental water samples using mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: In this study, mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) as a unique nanosorbent and N-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) as a cost-effective nanosensor were created and utilized, for the first time, for dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction (Dµ-SPE) to determine carmine (E120) dye in water samples and juices. The modification of the magnetic nanoparticles with mannitol was designed to enhance the responsive potential for adsorption of the polar E120 dye from complex sample matrices through electrostatic interaction. The as-fabricated N-CDs fluorescent probe exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of 43.1 %, allowing for accurate fluorometric detection of E120 dye. The as-synthesized MMNPs nanosorbent and fluorescent N-CDs nanoprobe were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM). Density functional theory (DFT) studied the E120 dye structure using Gaussian 09 to explore the interactions between E 120 dye molecules and MMNPs/N-CDs. The impact of the critical adsorption and detection experimental factors was investigated and adjusted. A minimal amount of MMNPs nanosorbent (150 mg) is sufficient for E120 extraction in an acceptable time of 15 min. Furthermore, with a high determination coefficient, the adsorption characteristics fit with the models of Langmuir isotherm and first-order kinetics. The maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of the as-fabricated MMNPs was 87.7 mg.g−1. After adsorption, E120 dye was fluorometrically analyzed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent nanosensor via the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed fluorometric procedures showed a linear increase in the fluorescence ratio with increasing the E120 concentration in the range of 1.0 – 160.0 μg.mL−1 with detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) limits of 0.27 and 0.83 μg.mL−1, respectively. The relative standard deviation (%RSD) did not exceed 2.34 %. The proposed methodology was successfully applied to determine E120 dye in juice and environmental water samples with % recovery ranged from 89.2-106.1 % and 92.9–107.2 %, respectively offering a reliable and environmentally friendly alternative to traditional detection methods with potential applications across various industries.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorometric sensing of polar E120 in juice and environmental water samples using mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) as a unique nanosorbent and N-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) as a cost-effective nanosensor were created and utilized, for the first time, for dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction (Dµ-SPE) to determine carmine (E120) dye in water samples and juices. The modification of the magnetic nanoparticles with mannitol was designed to enhance the responsive potential for adsorption of the polar E120 dye from complex sample matrices through electrostatic interaction. The as-fabricated N-CDs fluorescent probe exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of 43.1 %, allowing for accurate fluorometric detection of E120 dye. The as-synthesized MMNPs nanosorbent and fluorescent N-CDs nanoprobe were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM). Density functional theory (DFT) studied the E120 dye structure using Gaussian 09 to explore the interactions between E 120 dye molecules and MMNPs/N-CDs. The impact of the critical adsorption and detection experimental factors was investigated and adjusted. A minimal amount of MMNPs nanosorbent (150 mg) is sufficient for E120 extraction in an acceptable time of 15 min. Furthermore, with a high determination coefficient, the adsorption characteristics fit with the models of Langmuir isotherm and first-order kinetics. The maximum adsorption capacity (qm) of the as-fabricated MMNPs was 87.7 mg.g−1. After adsorption, E120 dye was fluorometrically analyzed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent nanosensor via the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed fluorometric procedures showed a linear increase in the fluorescence ratio with increasing the E120 concentration in the range of 1.0 – 160.0 μg.mL−1 with detection (LOD) and quantitation (LOQ) limits of 0.27 and 0.83 μg.mL−1, respectively. The relative standard deviation (%RSD) did not exceed 2.34 %. The proposed methodology was successfully applied to determine E120 dye in juice and environmental water samples with % recovery ranged from 89.2-106.1 % and 92.9–107.2 %, respectively offering a reliable and environmentally friendly alternative to traditional detection methods with potential applications across various industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Colored dyestuffs are commonly employed in different industries, such as cosmetics, drugs, textiles, and food products, and have stable aromatic structures and are difficult to biodegrade [1]. They are considered water pollutants in water and wastewater generated by various industries. It is essential to know that even a dye’s concentration as low as 1.0 ppm in water can significantly impact its properties and harm the environment and human health [2,3]. Among these colored dyestuffs, E120 dye is the most crucial food dye used to make food more attractive and appetizing [4]. It is used in the USA, Korea, Canada, and the European Union (EU) [5]. E120 dye is a bright-red dye obtained synthetically and naturally from the female Dactylopius coccus Costa insect [6]. It is broadly employed in various food products, such as yoghurt and juices, as well as in pharmaceutical formulations and cosmetics [7–10]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), its acceptable daily intake (ADI) value is 5 mg/kg based on weight [5]. Previous studies have reported that E120 dye significantly harms human health and food safety when consumed excessively or in amounts more significant than the ADI amounts, owing to its genotoxicity, cytostatic, and cytotoxic effects [11–13]. Therefore, monitoring E120 levels in high-consumption products such as food and beverages is extremely important [14,15]. To date, several methods have been reported for the assay of E120 dye in food samples, such as electrochemical [16,17], HPLC [18,19], and spectrophotometric [20] methods. Separation-based methods have many drawbacks, such as using hazardous organic solvents, high costs, highly skilled technicians, and multiple extractions. Even with the outstanding sensitivity of electrochemical sensing methods, poor selectivity and stability are the fundamental limitations that limit their quality control applicability. In recent years, adsorption technology has become a popular method for treating wastewater containing organic pollutants due to its cost-effectiveness, high efficiency, and environmental eco-friendliness [1]. Understanding the mechanisms of adsorption is crucial for designing effective adsorbents and optimizing the adsorption process. Among these mechanisms, adsorption isotherm models play a key role in explaining the relationship between the amount of adsorbate and its equilibrium concentration on the surface at a constant temperature [1,2]. Adsorption models include Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin isotherms. Langmuir model focuses on chemical adsorption on a uniform surface where adsorption ceases once all sites are occupied, while Freundlich model describes physical adsorption on heterogeneous surface. Temkin model, on the other hand, accounts for the interactions between the adsorbate and adsorbent [2]. Furthermore, sample extraction and preparation are currently shifted toward more cost-effective and user-friendly green methods. Sample preparation technology, including pre-concentration, extraction, and clean-up procedures, allows for the simultaneous analysis of multiple food additives with increased specificity and lower detection limits [21–23]. Recent reports have confirmed the problem's importance and underscored the need for rapid, precise, and selective dyestuffs analysis methods [24]. Dispersive magnetic micro-solid-phase extraction (DM-µ-SPE) is a low-cost, simple, and quick method for pre-concentrating, cleaning, and extracting various analytes from samples with limited volumes. This method offers high enrichment factors and is particularly suitable for samples with small sizes [25,26]. Different sorbent materials have been used for dye adsorption to purify and extract wastewater containing dyes with varying concentrations. Activated carbon (AC) has traditionally been employed as a sorbent material for wastewater treatment. However, its use has been restricted due to its expensive nature, limited selectivity, and the complexities associated with its regeneration processes. Numerous innovative alternatives have been developed to replace traditional activated carbon to enhance adsorption efficiency. One such alternative includes the utilization of nanosorbent materials [27,28]. Many nanosorbents are utilized to pre-concentrate and extract the analytes from their respective matrices [29–31]. Among these nanosorbents, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) could replace the traditional solid-phase extraction adsorbents because of their large surface area, high adsorption capacity, and high analyte selectivity.

Moreover, MNPs are easily isolated from the sample solution via an external magnet without classical separation methods [32–34]. Coating the surface of MNPs with polyhydroxy compounds like mannitol can result in improvedcolloidal stability and a greater ability to extract E120 dye due to its many hydroxyl groups and their ability to form hydrogen bonds [35]. Therefore, we hypothesize that the synthesized mannitol-functionalized magneticNPs could potentially adsorb and extract the polar E120 dye from their complex samples by forming multiple hydrogen bonds. Recently, N-CDs as nanomaterials have been extensively applied in fluorometric analysis because of their unique fluorescent merits, lower preparation costs, water solubility, environmental safety, and biocompatible properties [36–41]. Doping carbon dots with a heteroatom such as nitrogen will enhance their fluorescence properties in sensing [40–43].

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to document the use of mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) as nanosorbent for E120 extraction, combined with N-CDs as a fluorescent nanosensor. This combination enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of E120 detection, providing a comprehensive package for monitoring E120 dye levels in water samples. The novelty of this work lies in the development and application of MMNPs and N-CDs for the fluorometric determination of the hazardous E120 dye. This method is environmentally friendly and highly effective for detecting and removing E120 dye from aqueous solutions and real samples, offering a sustainable solution for monitoring E120 dye levels. Also, this study will evaluate the simulation study by performing (DFT) calculations for the behavior of the mechanism interactions between E 120 dye and the MMNPs/N-CDs with the experimental data.

In this study, all materials used were of analytical grade. Carmine dye (E120), mannitol, H2PO4, Na2HPO4, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, HNO3, NH4OH, citric acid, neutral red, urea, and ethanol were purchased from Loba chime. The phosphate buffer (PB) of pH 7.0 was prepared using analytical grade monobasic sodium phosphate and dibasic sodium phosphate, both with a purity ≥ 99 %, dissolved in deionized water. HCl and NaOH were utilized to adjust the pH to the required value. Double-distilled water (DDW) was used.

Spectrofluorometric (Agilent Carry 100 PC) instrumentation was utilized with excitation and emission slit controls set at 5 nm and 1 cm quartz cells. UV/VIS cintra 1010/GBC (Australia) was used to measure the light absorption. FT-IR spectra in the 400–4000 cm−1 range were recorded utilizing a NicoletTM iSTM10 spectrometer (Varian Instrument Co., Ltd, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the morphology of nanomaterials, and images were recorded utilizing the JSM-7600F for TEM and the JSM-5400LV for SEM (JEOL, USA). The elemental microanalysis was performed using the EDX technique (Oxford, Aztec apparatus, USA). XRD readings were examined using a Philips 1710 diffractometer from the USA. The TGA of the as-synthesized MNPs and MMNPs were performed using Rigaku (8120, Japan)Thermo plus EVO2 in an air flow atmosphere (10 °C. min−1). The magnetic properties of the as-synthesized MNPs and MMNPs were analyzed using a VSM (Lake Shore, USA).

A modified co-precipitation method was used to fabricate MMNP [35]. 1000 mg of FeCl2·4H2O and 3000 mg of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of DDW under stirring for 30 min and heated at 70 °C. Then, 15 mL of a 25 % NH3 solution was added to the mixture with constant stirring and maintained at 70 °C for 30 min. After that, 1000 mg of D-mannitol was dissolved in 3 ml of DDW, added to the mixture under stirring for 60 min, and heated at 90 °C to functionalize the MNPs with D-mannitol. The MNNPs were separated from the solution using an external magnetic field and then washed three times with DDW using the magnet. Subsequently, they were placed in an oven and dried at 60 °C for 3 hrs (Scheme 1 ).

A modified hydrothermal method was used to fabricate N-CDs [44]. 7000 mg and 10 mg of citric acid and neutral red, respectively, were dissolved in 25 ml of DDW with magnetic stirring for 5 min to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was heated at 180 °C for 4.5 hrs. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected by using filtration at a 0.22 µm pore size. Finally, the filtered solution was dried to obtain purified solid N-CDs (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the DM-µ-SPE method
ce:section.ce:para: One hundred fifty milligrams of MMNPs were added to 10 ml of E120 solution in the concentration range (1–20 µg.mL−1) in centrifuge tubes and 2 ml of PB solution of pH 7.0. The solution was vortexed for 15 min. The MMNPs were separated from the solution using an external magnet. To elute E120 dye from the MMNPs, 5 mL of methanol was added to them and then sonicated for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of E120 dye
ce:section.ce:para: The eluted E120 dye solution from the previous procedure was mixed with 2.0 mL of N-CDs solution and 1.0 mL of PB (pH 7), and the mixture was diluted to 10.0 mL with DDW. The absorbance and fluorescence absorbance spectra were measured after 15 min at λex 580 nm and λex/λem at 623/530 nm, respectively.

Scheme 1 provides a summary of the overall methodology. Step I involves the DM-µ-SPE method, and Step II shows the synthesis of fluorescent N-CDs, which are subsequently used for the determination of E120 via a dynamic quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of adsorption isotherms of E120 dye
ce:section.ce:para:
Adsorption of E120 on MMNPs was studied using different E120 concentrations (1–20 µg.mL−1) with a fixed amount of MMNPs (150 mg), and 10 ml of BP solution of pH 7.0 at various temperatures (25, 30, 40, and 50 °C) and vortexed for 15 min. The remaining E120 dye in the solution was determined fluorometrically at λex/λem at 623/530 nm following mixing with 750 µg.mL−1 of N-CDs. The difference in concentration between the initial ( C ° , µg.mL−1) and equilibrium (Ce, µg.mL−1) concentrations that remained in the solution was then calculated (Eq. (1)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The Φ of the as-synthesized N-CDs is calculated via the following formula:
(1)
Φ s = Φ s t
I x
A x
A s t
I s t
η x
η s t

Where Φ, I, A, η, and the subscripts “st” and “x” represent the corresponding quantum yield, adjusted fluorescence intensity at the selected excitation wavelength, absorbance intensity, solvent refractive index, reference, and the sample, respectively. Quinine sulfate (0.1 M in H2SO4) represents the reference solution.

Real samples of tap water, mineral water and juice were used. Tap water was obtained from our laboratory (Najran University, Najran, KSA). Bottled mineral water and juice samples were purchased from local Najran, KSA, markets. The samples were collected in cleaned polyethylene bottles. Only the juice sample was filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size membrane filter immediately to remove the suspending particles before storing at 4 ◦C. Finally, samples were prepared by spiking 10 mL aliquots of these samples with different E120 concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80, 100 and 150 µg.mL−1) before applying the recommended procedure for the DM-µ-SPE method. E120 dye concentration in the commercial juice and real water samples was first predetermined.

The extraction recovery percentage (% ER) was employed to assess the extraction efficiency to achieve optimal conditions, ER represents the proportion of the total analyte (P°) extracted into the extraction phase (Pex), and it can be computed using the formula [45]:
% E R =
P e x
P o
X 100
The ER ranged from 91.8 to 95.6 %, suggesting the efficiency of the solid phase nanoparticles in extracting E120 dye.

EDX analysis was utilized to identify the elemental composition of the as-synthesized MMNPs as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The three peaks at (1.25, 7, 0.5 keV) are related to the binding energies of Fe and O, respectively [35]. The absence of the C peak is probably due to the carbonization process, which turns into carbon dioxide and evaporates, and an oxygen peak appears. The FT-IR technique was employed to determine the essential functional groups of MMNPs. Four peaks appeared in Fig. 1b at 3210, 1834, 1627, and 621 cm−1, corresponding to (OH), (C = O), (C–H), and (Fe-O), respectively [46,47]. Fig. 1C displays the XRD pattern of magnetic nanoparticles and mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles. Fig. 1C(a) shows that the magnetic nanoparticles exhibit diffraction peaks at 31.2°, 38.7°, 43.2°, 46.8°, 55.6°, and 78.9°, assigning to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) diffraction planes of magnetic nanoparticles [35,48]. These diffraction peaks were not altered after modification with mannitol, as depicted in Fig. 1C (b), owing to the chemical stability of MNPs [35]. The SEM and TEM images are displayed in Fig. 1d and 1e, respectively, illustrate that MMNPs are spherical with an average size of 20 nm [35]. Fig. 1f demonstrates the TGA curves of magnetic nanoparticles and mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles. The initial weight loss for magnetic nanoparticles up to 132 °C can be attributed to the removal of surface adsorbed water. The weight loss the temperature range of 235–283 °C can be attributed to removing chemisorbed organic molecules [35], as seen in Fig. 1f (a). For mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles Fig. 1f(b), another weight loss at a temperature range of 335–388 °C can be attributed to the removal of mannitol from the surface of magnetic nanoparticles [49]. Fig. 1g displays the VSM of magnetic nanoparticles and mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles. It was found that about 12.45 % decreases in the magnetization value for mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles, which can be attributed to the existence of non-magnetic organic coating i.e. mannitol [50].

The as-created N-CDs have a prominent absorption peak at 530 nm and a characteristic fluorescence emission peak at 623 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. Moreover, we could determine a clear spectral overlap between the absorption band of E120 dye and the fluorescence emission of N-CDs, implying a quenching process due to the IFE [51]. TEM images were employed to examine the morphology of the as-created N-CDs. Fig. 2b showed that N-CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed, and the average diameter of the particle size distribution is 3.5 nm. FT-IR was used to identify the functional groups on the N-CDs surface as shown in Fig. 2c, which indicates an abundance of oxygen at three distinct peaks (3457, 1775, and 1333 cm−1) corresponding to the vibrations of (O–H, C = O, and C-O-C), respectively. In addition, the presence of C − H and C = C bonds were confirmed by the two peaks at 3025 and 1583 cm−1, respectively [44]. Fig. 2d showed a decrease in emission intensity at pH values lower or higher than 7.0, which may be due to the protonation state of the surface functional groups. In lower pH, the high acidity causes protonation of the amine and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of N-CDs. It produces ammonium conjugate acid and carbonization of carboxylic acid groups. The protons are neutralized at a higher pH in the alkaline condition [52]. The effect of temperature on the emission intensity of N-CDs has also been examined; Fig. 2e shows a decrease in emission intensity by increasing the temperature owing to the increased activated collisions due to the acceleration of molecular movement, which reduces fluorescence emission. Furthermore, the effect of ionic salt concentration was also tested. Fig. 2f showed decreased emission intensity with increasing the ionic concentration from 0.1 to 2.0 M. The reduction in emission intensity may be due to the electrostatic attraction between the salt ions and the N-CDs surface. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of the synthesized N-CDs is calculated with a quinine sulphate reference standard. The hydrothermal method was successfully utilized to synthesize N-CDs with about (Φs) = 43.1 % at λex = 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence sensor affecting variables
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best sensor sensitivity and consequently the highest sensitivity of the proposed method, three essential factors affecting the emission intensity of N-CDs were studied after incubating them with E120 dye, such as pH, contact time, and diluting solvent. Fig. 3 a showed a decrease in emission intensity at pH values lower than or higher than pH 7.0. This may be attributed to the pH effect on the N-CDs surface functional groups and the formation of a protective barrier on the N-CDs surface owing to the electrostatic interaction [52]. The formation of a protective barrier on the surface of N-CDs in an aqueous solution may be due to electrostatic interaction between N-CDs and H+ or OH– ions. This barrier acts as a physical shield, influencing the surface properties and interactions of the N-CDs. However, the N-CDs might be sensitive to changes in pH, which can affect their stability and fluorescence properties. Therefore, a buffer solution of pH 7.0 was used after that. Fig. 3b shows the effect of contact time on the emission intensity of N-CDs. The results showed that the emission intensity of N-CDs was stable after 20 min. The effect of different organic solvents (DDW, ethanol, methanol, and tetrahydrofuran) on the emission intensity of the N-CDs was also examined, as shown in Fig. 3c. The emission intensity of N-CDs appeared to be higher when employing DDW than the other solvents due to the free dispersion of N-CDs in water.

Sundry circumstances influencing the E120 dye adsorption on MMNPs were examined to realize the maximum adsorption capacity (qe), such as MMNPs amount, pH, contact time, ionic strength, and temperature. To develop a practical procedure for E120 dye extraction and removal from complicated matrices, various amounts of the as-fabricated nanosorbent (25–250 mg) were investigated to attain the optimum amount of MMNPs. Fig. 4 a shows a gradual increase in E120 dye adsorption by increasing the MMNPs mass up to the maximum adsorption at 100 mg, after which the qe was unaffected. As a result, 150 mg was selected as the optimum mass for further investigation [53,54]. Because of our prior knowledge of the importance of pH in the adsorption process, various pH values (1.0––10.2) were examined for their effect on E120 dye adsorption, and the results are represented in Fig. 4b. The maximum E120 adsorption was found at pH 7. In an acidic medium, low pH decreased the ionization and adsorption of E120 dye, which may be attributed to decreasing the electrostatic attraction on MMNPs surfaces [54,55]. In an alkaline medium, the MMNPs surface functional groups are ionized and electrostatic repulsion occurs to impregnate the E120 dye adsorption on MMNPs [54–56]. So, a buffer solution with a pH of 7.0 was the most favourable. In addition, contact time plays a significant role in the adsorbent-adsorbate process. Fig. 4c showed that the E120 dye adsorption onto MMNPs increased with increasing the contact time from 5 to 15 min and then slightly increased with time until it reached the highest adsorption at equilibrium. As time progresses, the number of free active sites on MMNPs surfaces decreases, making it difficult for E120 dye to find free adsorption sites [57,58]. Thus, 15 min was the optimum contact time for further experiments. The ionic strength of the solution could affect E120 adsorption on MMNPs since E120 comprises several distinct polar groups. So, the effect of ionic strength at varied concentrations (0.05 to 2 M) on E120 dye adsorption onto MMNPs was examined, and the results are represented in Fig. 4d. The extraction increased with increasing the ionic strength from 0.05 to 0.1 M and then slightly decreased as the concentration increased. The result could be due to a less favorable electrostatic interaction between the E120 dye and MMNPs or to competition between the E120 dye and ions for adsorption sites on the surface of MMNPs [8,57]. Furthermore, the effect of temperature (25, 30, 40, and 50 ◦C) on the E120 extraction using MMNPs was examined. Fig. 4e showed a decrease in the E120 extraction by increasing temperature, which may be due to decreasing the electrostatic attraction between the active sites in MMNPs and E120 dye [59]. When the temperature increases, the active adsorption sites in MMNPs are destroyed, this decreases the electrostatic attraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QTAIM analysis for elucidating E120-MMNPs interaction mode
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a Quantum Theory of Atom in Molecule (QTAIM) analysis was conducted to elucidate the nature of the interaction between E120 dye molecules and MMNPs. The application of QTAIM analysis provided us with valuable insights into these interactions [60–62]. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 software package at the B3LYP/6–31 g(d,p) level of theory [63]. This enabled the identification of optimal structures for the E120 dye molecule, MMNPs, N-CDs, and the complexes produced by the E120 dye with MMNPs and N-CDs. The optimized structures are depicted in Fig. S2. Multiple orientations of E120/MMNPs and E120/N-CDs complexes were examined to explore various interactions. As a result, three stable configurations were identified for the E120/MMNPs complex, as shown in Fig. S3, and four stable configurations were found for the E120/N-CDs complex, as shown in Fig. S4. The Multiwfn 3.7 software package was utilized to predict and visualize the bond critical points (BCPs) of type (3,-1) formed between the constituents of each complex [64]. Electrostatic interactions such as van der Waals interactions, weak hydrogen bonds, and ionic bonds can be characterized using the following specific criteria; −G(r)/V(r) > 1, H(r) > 0, and ∇2ρ > 0 [60–62]. Strong interactions were categorized by ∇2ρ > 10 – 1 au, weak interactions by ∇2ρ < 10 – 1 au, and partially covalent bonds by ∇2ρ > 0 and 0.5 < -G(r)/V(r) < 1. Celik and Tanıs [61] reported that strong hydrogen bonds are characterized by ∇2ρ < 0, H(r) > 0, moderate hydrogen bonds by ∇2ρ > 0, H(r) < 0, and weak hydrogen bonds by ∇2ρ > 0, H(r) > 0. Fig. S3 illustrates the bond critical points (BCPs) formed between MMNPs and E120 dye molecules in three interaction modes denoted (a, b, and c). All these BCPs involve H-bonding interactions between the O-atom of one molecule and the H-atom of the other molecule. This observation suggests that MMNPs-E120 interaction in these specific modes involves hydrogen bonding interactions. According to Table S1, for the interaction mode a, BCPs 1 and 2 have ∇2ρ values of 0.128 and 0.085 au, respectively, indicating MMNPs-E120 interaction owing to partial covalent bonds. In modes b and c, BCP 1 has ∇2ρ values of 0.071 and 0.072 au (∇2ρ > 0) and also H(r) < 0 for (−0.0001 and −0.0001), respectively, indicating moderate H-bond formation. On the other hand, BCP 2 in interaction modes b and c has ∇2ρ > 0 (0.053 and 0.054 au) and H(r) > 0 for (0.0007 and 0.0007 au), respectively, indicating the formation of weak H-bond. These results suggested the formation of H-bonding through MMNPs-E120 interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the fluorescence sensor mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
QTAIM analysis was conducted to elucidate the interaction mechanism between E120 and N-CDs. Application of QTAIM analysis resulted in Fig.S3, which presents multiple BCPs formed between N-CDs and E120 dye molecules in four interaction modes (a, b, c, and d). Each mode exhibits specific BCPs that represent distinct interactions. According to Table S2, the −G(r)/V(r) ratios for BCPs 2 and 4 in mode a, BCPs 1 and 3 in mode b, BCPs 2 and 4 in mode c, and BCP 1 in mode d are all greater than 0.5 but less than 1, indicating partially covalent bonds. The remaining BCPs have −G(r)/V(r) ratios greater than 1, indicating pure electrostatic interactions. BCPs 1, 3, and 5 in mode a, BCPs 1 and 3 in mode c, and BCPs 2 and 4 in mode d have ∇2ρ > 0 and H(r) > 0, indicating weak H- bond formation. BCP 2 in mode b and BCP 3 in mode d have ∇2ρ > 0 and H(r) < 0, suggesting the formation of moderate H-bonds. Also, according to the fluorescence and absorption spectra shown in Fig. 2a, which show an overlap in the spectral range between the absorption band of E120 dye and the emission and excitation spectra of N-CDs which suggests a quenching mechanism due to the inner-filter effect (IFE) [65]. Therefore, we suggested both IFE and electrostatic interactions are the modes of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence ratio of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different E120 amounts was determined at 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C to distinguish if a static or dynamic mechanism of quenching of N-CDs is present. This can be calculated based on the Stern–Volmer (SV) Equation

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of thermodynamics of E120 extraction process
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrostatic interaction is expected to occur during E120 extraction by MMNPs. As a result, E120 adsorption onto MMNPs is expected to be associated with variable parameters such as the enthalpy, entropy, and free energy of the adsorption process. As a result, estimating the values of these parameters was significant. Under optimal experimental circumstances, E120 adsorption on MMNPs was examined, and the parameters free energy (
Δ G a d s
), enthalpy (
Δ H a d s
), and entropy (
Δ S a d s
) were calculated by applying the plot

Where R and T are constant gas (8.314 J.mol-1.K), and temperature (Kelvin), respectively. Table 2 showed the calculated thermodynamic parameters for E120 adsorption. The values of the parameters
Δ H a d s

, and

were (−34.5 kJ.mol−1), (0.07 kJ.mol−1 K−1), and

(−54.8, −55.1, −55.8, −55.5 KJ.mol−1) at different temperatures (298, 303, 313, 323 K), respectively. E120 adsorption onto MMNPs is exothermic process, with decreased randomness at the solid/liquid interface during the sorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of kinetic model of E120 extraction using MMNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the rate and mechanism of E120 extraction by MMNPs, kinetic models of E120 adsorption were examined. Therefore, the E120 adsorption on MNNPs at varying times (5–30 min) was examined using the pseudo-first order (PFO) and pseudo-second order (PSO) equations, as shown in Table 3 . The correlation coefficient (R2), qcal, and qexp values for PFO and PSO models were (0.960 and 0.9956), (0.5 and 75.2 mg.g−1), and (67.4 mg.g−1), respectively, as shown in Table 3. The experimental data confirmed that the PSO model was the appropriate model for E120 adsorption onto MMNPs [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of E120 adsorption on MMNPs with other adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para:
The value of the maximum adsorption capacity
( q m )
of E120 dye on the as-created nanosorbent MMNPs was compared to that of different adsorbents. Table 4 shows some reports in the literature that have been conducted using different adsorbents for E120 dye. The MMNPs are more effective for E120 dye removal from aqueous solutions, as shown in Table 4.

Under optimal experimental circumstances of the designed DM-µ-SPE procedure, the fluorescence intensity of the as-created N-CDs decreased with increasing E120 amount. (Fig. S6) showed a calibration plot of different concentrations of E120 dye vs. the corresponding F°/F ratios was constructed (n = 6). The R2 of the calibration plot was 0.9933, with a good linear range (1.0–160.0 µg mL−1). LOD (0.27 µg mL−1) and LOQ (0.83 µg.mL−1) were calculated based on the formulas LOD =
3 (
S D
S
) and LOQ =
10 (
S D
S
), respectively. (SD) of the intercept was calculated to be 0.032 while the slope (S) of the calibration plot was found to be 0.004. The reproducibility of the proposed DM-µ-SPE procedure was tested over three days by monitoring the fluorescence quenching produced by 100 µg mL−1 of E120 dye on the N-CDs emission intensity. The results revealed that %RSD did not exceed 2.3 %. These results confirmed the efficiency of the fluorescence nanosensor for the determination of E120 as well as the accuracy and precision of the proposed method in accordance with ICH guidelines [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of MMNPS to remove E120 dye
ce:section.ce:para: The MMNPs were effectively isolated using an external magnetic field and then washed three times with 5 mL of methanol. The MMNPs were reused for the subsequent extraction cycle, demonstrating their reusability and cost-effectiveness [72]. The reusability of MMNPs to extract E120 dye several times was examined. The results showed the ability of MMNPs to adsorb E120 dye after six cycles (98.6 %, 93.1 %, 90.5 %, 86.7 %, 78.3 %, and 68.9 %), respectively, as represented in (Fig. S7).

The efficiency of the proposed analytical method was verified by its applicability to determine the E120 dye in complicated matrices such as commercial juices, mineral water, and tap water. Table 5 showed that the % recovery ranged from 106.1–89.2 %, 107.2–93.9 %, and 102.4–92.9 % in commercial juice, mineral water, and tap water, respectively, demonstrating that the method is accurate within the desired range.

Herein, a sensitive and simple DM-µ-SPE method was successfully developed to extract and determine E120 dye in real samples. The method utilized the as-synthesized mannitol-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles for the efficient extraction of E120 via hydrogen bonding which was confirmed by the performed DFT studies. Additionally, the as-synthesized N-CDs were employed as nanofluorescent probe with high fluorescence quantum yield for fluorometric determination of E120. The combination of N-CDs and MMNPs significantly enhanced the sensitivity, selectivity, and applicability of the method for real-world sample analysis. The adsorption behavior is aligned with the Langmuir isotherm and first-order kinetics. This novel approach offers a sustainable efficient alternative to the conventional methods with potential applications.
